common_name: Native Yellow Canna (Bandana of the Everglades)

planting.note: Divide rhizomes and plant in boggy, shallow water (0–6 inches deep) or in wet, poorly drained soil after last frost; native yellow canna is a true wetland plant adapted to standing water and does not perform as well in ordinary garden beds. It is a parent of many modern canna hybrids and grows as a perennial in zones 8–11.

directions: Divide rhizomes and plant in boggy or shallow-water conditions in full sun; native yellow canna is the sole wild canna species of the southeastern United States, growing naturally in wetlands, pond margins, roadside ditches, and wet meadows from South Carolina to Florida and across the Gulf Coast to Texas. The soft-yellow, iris-like flowers are more delicate and translucent than the bold tropical cannas of cultivation, producing a graceful fluttering display on tall, narrow plants. It is an essential plant for native rain gardens, bioswales, pond edges, and wet meadow gardens in the Gulf Coast region, where it grows as a reliable perennial and provides larval food for Brazilian skipper butterflies. Unlike garden hybrid cannas, native canna self-sows and naturalizes in appropriate wet conditions. It was a primary parent of modern ornamental cannas — most cultivated cannas carry its genes.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant to moist to wet, fertile soil after last frost; Hibiscus grandiflorus is a wetland species that thrives in consistently moist soil and tolerates periodic flooding. Nick or soak seeds before planting to improve germination. In zones 8–11 it grows as a persistent perennial; in cooler zones it is killed by frost but may reseed.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, fertile soil in full sun; swamp rose mallow is the most dramatic of the native hibiscus species, producing enormous flowers — among the largest of any hardy perennial — reaching 8–12 inches across in blush pink with a deep crimson center eye, on plants that can reach 10 feet in ideal moist conditions. The large leaves are softly velvety-pubescent on the undersides, giving the plant its common name velvet mallow. Native to freshwater marshes, tidal flats, and pond margins in the Florida Peninsula and Gulf Coast, it is one of the showiest native wildflowers of the Southeast and a hummingbird magnet. The flowers bloom from June through September and the large seed capsules that follow are decorative into fall. It is more heat-tolerant and moisture-demanding than the closely related rose mallow (H. moscheutos) and is the best choice for wet sites in zone 8–11.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or fall directly in well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; meadow clary self-sows prolifically and naturalizes readily in meadow and prairie-style plantings. It flowers more prolifically in lean, dry, well-drained soils than in rich, moist ones.

directions: Sow in well-drained, neutral to alkaline, lean soil in full sun; meadow clary is a robust European native perennial producing abundant spikes of vivid violet-blue flowers in late spring and early summer — smaller than some of the garden salvias but produced in great quantities on branching plants that naturalize freely in meadows, prairie gardens, and gravel plantings. The crinkled, wrinkled basal leaves form attractive rosettes even out of bloom. Unlike the cultivated Salvia x sylvestris hybrids (S. nemorosa crosses), meadow clary is a true species that flowers from seed the first year and self-sows to form permanent colonies. The 'Haematodes' selection produces unusually large flowers of clear blue. Excellent for dry, chalky, alkaline soils where many other ornamentals struggle. One of the best plants for pollinators in the perennial garden, visited constantly by bumblebees, honeybees, and hoverflies throughout its long bloom season.

planting.note: Sow seeds in spring or plant tuberous roots 3–4 inches deep after last frost; Incarvillea is tap-rooted and resents transplanting — sow direct or in deep pots that can be transplanted without root disturbance. The fleshy roots store energy through winter but are not frost-hardy in poorly drained or wet soils; mulch heavily in zones 5–6.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; Chinese trumpet flower is one of the most exotic-looking hardy perennials for temperate gardens, producing large, vivid rose-pink to magenta, trumpet-shaped flowers that look exactly like tropical bignonia or mandevilla — 2–3 inch wide tubular blooms in brilliant color — on compact plants with attractive, pinnately compound leaves. The combination of tropical flower size and shape with genuine hardiness to zone 5 is remarkable. Plants emerge late in spring (mark their location to avoid disturbing the dormant roots) and produce a concentrated flush of flowers in June, after which the plant rests. It requires excellent drainage through winter to prevent root rot. I. mairei is a more compact, earlier-blooming species; I. olgae is taller with smaller, profuse pink flowers excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer with the neck of the bulb just at soil level in very well-drained, sandy soil in full sun; sea daffodil requires extremely well-drained, sandy or gritty soil and must have dry summer dormancy — wet summer conditions cause bulb rot. It flowers best when crowded and pot-bound, so avoid frequent division.

directions: Plant bulbs in sharply drained, sandy soil in full sun; sea daffodil is a stunning Mediterranean coastal bulb growing on sandy beaches and dunes from the Atlantic coast of Portugal through the Mediterranean basin, producing pure white, sweetly fragrant flowers resembling a large, elaborate spider lily in late summer — three to twelve large, pure-white, sweetly scented flowers per stem with frilly, fringed petals and a prominent cup. The broad, strap-shaped, glaucous blue-gray leaves are themselves ornamental and persist through the growing season. It thrives in exactly the conditions found in Gulf Coast beach gardens: sandy, well-drained, alkaline, saline soils baked by summer sun. After summer dormancy, the flower stems emerge in August and September before the new season's foliage. It is quite different from the native Hymenocallis (spider lilies) despite the similar flower shape and is an excellent companion to Mediterranean herbs and other drought-tolerant bulbs.
